sections.heading: What Makes a Complete Sentence
sections.explanation: Every complete sentence MUST have: 1. A SUBJECT (who/what the sentence is about), 2. A VERB (what the subject does/is), 3. A COMPLETE THOUGHT (makes sense alone).

fragments.description: A fragment is missing something. It feels unfinished.

fixes: Because she was tired, she went to bed.
She left early because she was tired.
Although it was raining, we went outside.
We stayed home although it was raining.

sections.complete_thought_test: Read the words out loud. Does the listener feel like waiting for more? YES = It's a FRAGMENT (add more!). NO = It's COMPLETE ✅

sections.heading: Types of Sentences
sections.explanation: There are 4 types of sentences in English. Each serves a different purpose.

types.name: Simple Sentence
types.description: One subject + one verb = one complete thought
examples: Dogs bark.
She laughed.
The teacher explained the lesson clearly.
My younger brother plays football every evening.
The beautiful sunset painted the sky orange.

types.name: Compound Sentence
types.description: Two simple sentences joined by FANBOYS + comma OR joined by a semicolon
examples: I love reading, and she loves writing.
He was tired, but he kept working.
You can take the bus, or you can walk.
She studied hard, so she passed the test.
It was cold; we stayed inside.
He wanted to go; she wanted to stay.

types.name: Complex Sentence
types.description: One main sentence + one dependent clause (joined by subordinating conjunctions)
examples: I went to bed because I was tired.
Although it was raining, we went outside.
She will succeed if she works hard.
When the bell rang, the students left.
He waited until she arrived.
Because she studied hard, she passed.
types.note: Pattern B (dependent clause FIRST) needs a comma. Pattern A does NOT.

types.name: Compound-Complex Sentence
types.description: Two main clauses + one dependent clause
examples: Although it was raining, I went outside, and I got completely wet.
Because she worked hard, she got promoted, and her team celebrated her success.
When the meeting ended, John went home, but Mary stayed to finish the report.

why_mix.only_simple_example: I woke up. I brushed my teeth. I ate breakfast. I went to school. I came home. I did homework. (boring and choppy)
why_mix.mixed_example: I woke up early and brushed my teeth quickly. After eating a light breakfast, I went to school. When I came home, I was tired, but I still finished all my homework. (natural and engaging)

sections.heading: Run-On Sentences
sections.explanation: A run-on sentence happens when two or more complete sentences are joined INCORRECTLY.

types.name: Fused Sentence
types.description: No punctuation at all between sentences
types.wrong: I love dogs I have three dogs they are all different breeds.
fixes: I love dogs. I have three dogs. They are all different breeds. (Make separate sentences)
I love dogs, and I have three of them, all different breeds. (Combine with comma + FANBOYS)
I love dogs; I have three, all different breeds. (Use semicolon)

types.name: Comma Splice
wrong_examples: I was hungry, I ordered pizza.
She studied all night, she still failed.
It was getting late, we had to leave.
fix_options: I was hungry, so I ordered pizza. (Add FANBOYS after comma)
I was hungry. I ordered pizza. (Make two sentences)
I was hungry; I ordered pizza. (Use semicolon)
Because I was hungry, I ordered pizza. (Make complex sentence)

practice_fixes.wrong: The weather was perfect we decided to go for a walk it was really enjoyable.
practice_fixes.right: The weather was perfect, so we decided to go for a walk. It was really enjoyable.

practice_fixes.wrong: She loves cooking, she makes amazing Italian food, her pasta is the best.
practice_fixes.right: She loves cooking and makes amazing Italian food. Her pasta is the best.

practice_fixes.wrong: I called him three times he didn't answer I was worried about him.
practice_fixes.right: I called him three times, but he didn't answer. I was worried about him.

sections.heading: Sentence Variety & Flow
sections.explanation: Good writing uses a MIX of sentence lengths. Short sentences = impact and clarity. Long sentences = detail and connection.

comparison.all_short: I went to Paris. It was beautiful. I saw the Eiffel Tower. I ate croissants. I loved it. I want to go back. Paris is amazing. (choppy and boring)
comparison.all_long: When I visited the magnificent city of Paris last summer, which is known for its incredible beauty and stunning architecture, I was able to see the famous Eiffel Tower, which is one of the most recognized structures in the world, and I also had the pleasure of eating authentic French croissants, which were absolutely delicious, and I truly loved every moment of the trip... (exhausting to read)
comparison.mixed: Paris took my breath away. As I walked through its elegant streets, I felt like I was inside a painting. The Eiffel Tower stood magnificent against the golden sky, and the croissants melted on my tongue. I fell in love with the city. I will definitely return. (natural and engaging)

sentences.text: Running to the bus stop every morning.
sentences.fix: She runs to the bus stop every morning.

sentences.text: I love cooking I make Italian food my friends love it.
sentences.fix: I love cooking and make Italian food. My friends love it.
She called, but he didn't answer.

tasks.instruction: Write one of each sentence type:
requirements: One paragraph (5-6 sentences, mix all types) - Topic: Describe your favorite place
